retrieved_docs.title: Vesicoureteral Reflux -- Technique or Treatment -- Predictors of Successful Endoscopic Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Another factor to consider is the specific injection technique utilized. Following the initial learning period, the HIT injection method appears to be superior to the STING or modified STING procedures, with success rates of 89% versus 71%. However, some studies have shown no significant difference between the 2 techniques. The surgeon's experience with endoscopic injections is an independent predictor of the final success rate. The initial 110 cases show a clear learning curve, with gradual improvement until the success rate stabilizes. Proficiency is typically achieved after at least 20 cases. Despite being generally regarded as a straightforward procedure, technical expertise and experience significantly influence outcomes, especially in complex cases like higher-grade VUR and ureteral duplications. Increasing the amount of bulking material typically enhances the final success rate but also leads to higher costs.

retrieved_docs.title: Vesicoureteral Reflux -- Technique or Treatment -- Endoscopic Treatment of Vesicoureteral Reflux
retrieved_docs.content: The endoscopic approach offers numerous benefits, including generally favorable outcomes, minimal invasiveness, limited tissue dissection or harm, low morbidity, outpatient availability, and ease of repeated use if needed. However, the injection technique and bulking agent volume are crucial. Insufficient material may not reduce the reflux, while excessive amounts can lead to ureteral obstruction and hydronephrosis. Younger age, higher reflux grade, and previous unsuccessful endoscopic antireflux procedures are negative predictors of successful reflux resolution via endoscopy. [155]

retrieved_docs.title: Vesicoureteral Reflux -- Technique or Treatment -- Predictors of Successful Endoscopic Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Endoscopic periureteral bulking therapy has shown better outcomes under various conditions and circumstances. The grade of VUR significantly influences the outcome and is the most crucial factor in predicting successful endoscopic surgery, according to one meta-analysis. Reported success rates in this analysis were as follows: Grade I: 89% Grade II: 83% Grade III: 71% Grade IV: 59% Grade V: 62% A recent study involving over 850 cases of VUR treated endoscopically demonstrated resolution in 70.4% of grade IV cases and 61.9% of grade V cases after a single periureteral injection procedure. Recurrent or residual reflux was resolved in an additional 20.1% after a second injection and 10.4% more after a third injection.

retrieved_docs.title: Vesicoureteral Reflux -- Technique or Treatment -- Predictors of Successful Endoscopic Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: A lower number of previous endoscopic surgical procedures is associated with a higher overall success rate. Successful outcomes correlate with proper mound configuration and appearance. The presence of bowel or bladder dysfunction negatively predicts VUR control after endoscopic surgery. Untreated abnormal bladder function significantly increases VUR recurrence. Preoperative bladder dysfunction is not a contraindication to endoscopic surgery but should be addressed before surgical treatment. Endoscopic treatment is more likely to fail in patients aged younger than 1 and those with existing renal scarring. Endoscopic treatment success rates are enhanced by administering larger volumes of periureteral bulking agents and employing multiple injection sites, particularly at high-volume centers of excellence.

retrieved_docs.title: Vesicoureteral Reflux -- Technique or Treatment -- Techniques of Endoscopic Vesicorureteral Surgery
retrieved_docs.content: A follow-up evaluation, including renal ultrasonography and a VCUG, is recommended 3 months after the procedure. If the original procedure fails, a second endoscopic procedure can be performed regardless of the initial technique. Overall reported success rates with multiple injections in high-volume centers are about 85% but vary from 70% to 100%. [167] Recurrent reflux is not uncommon within 1 year after treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Vesicoureteral Reflux -- Technique or Treatment -- Techniques of Endoscopic Vesicorureteral Surgery
retrieved_docs.content: Another modification of the STING technique called "ureteral repositioning and injection" begins with the same insertion of the injection needle as the standard STING procedure. However, the needle insertion raises and elevates the distal intraluminal ureter toward the bladder lumen, after which the bulking agent is injected. This variation requires significantly less bulking agent (average of 0.4 versus 0.7 mL), the procedure is simple to perform, and the reported success rate for resolution of VUR is improved compared to the standard STING surgery, up to 91%. [162]

retrieved_docs.title: Vesicoureteral Reflux -- Technique or Treatment -- Endoscopic Treatment of Vesicoureteral Reflux
retrieved_docs.content: Dextranomere-hyaluronic acid copolymer: Introduced in 2002, the dextranomere-hyaluronic acid copolymer is a viscous gel containing dextranomere microspheres in a hyaluronic acid carrier base. Other features are listed below. The microspheres have an intermediate size (80-250 μm in diameter). The material is non-biodegradable and appears immunologically inert. This material causes minimal scarring and has no apparent malignancy-promoting properties. A minimal histological reaction is not apparent to the injected material. The copolymer is less likely to cause ureteral obstruction over time. The typical injected volume is about 1 mL (ranging from 0.5 to 1.5 mL) and is slightly higher on average than that of polyacrylate polyalcohol.

retrieved_docs.title: Vesicoureteral Reflux -- Technique or Treatment -- Techniques of Endoscopic Vesicorureteral Surgery
retrieved_docs.content: HIT method: The HIT method, introduced by Kirsch in 2004, utilizes hydrostatic pressure to distend the intramural ureteral lumen, facilitating improved visualization. The injection needle is placed within the lumen of the intramural ureter at the 6 o'clock position before traversing the ureter's posterior wall into the submucosal space, where the bulking agent is injected to form a volcano-shaped mound. This technique allows for more optimal placement of the bulking agent due to enhanced needle accuracy, resulting in an augmented antireflux effect within the intramural ureter, extending beyond primarily at the orifice.

retrieved_docs.title: Vesicoureteral Reflux -- Technique or Treatment -- Endoscopic Treatment of Vesicoureteral Reflux
retrieved_docs.content: The typical injected volume is about 1 mL (ranging from 0.5-1.5 mL). The copolymer may offer better long-term outcomes compared to dextranomer-hyaluronic acid. The copolymer may cause ureteral obstruction over time; thus, long-term follow-up is therefore suggested. [159] [160]

retrieved_docs.title: Vesicoureteral Reflux -- Technique or Treatment -- Endoscopic Treatment of Vesicoureteral Reflux
retrieved_docs.content: Bulking agents currently used for endoscopic antireflux surgery include dextranomer-hyaluronic acid gel and polyacrylate-polyalcohol copolymer, which have been used as injectable bulking agents for over a decade, especially for lower-grade VUR, yielding similar outcomes. [156] Although dextranomer-hyaluronic acid copolymer boasts a slightly superior initial success rate, it carries a higher risk of ureteral obstruction and a significant incidence of late reflux recurrence.

retrieved_docs.title: Vesicoureteral Reflux -- Technique or Treatment -- Techniques of Endoscopic Vesicorureteral Surgery
retrieved_docs.content: Since then, the standard technique has been modified by advancing the tip of the injection needle 4 to 5 mm after insertion, positioning it within the submucosa of the intramural ureter. Injection of the bulking agent elevates the distal intramural ureter, forming a mound that stretches and elongates the UVJ while angulating the ureteral meatus. [161]

retrieved_docs.title: Vesicoureteral Reflux -- Technique or Treatment -- Techniques of Endoscopic Vesicorureteral Surgery
retrieved_docs.content: The method is often modified to the double HIT technique, where both proximal and distal intraluminal submucosal ureteral injections are performed to improve the antireflux effect further by modifying the entire length of the intramural ureter. The first or more proximal injection is intended to cause coaptation along the UVJ while the bulking compound traverses the ureter, causing partial lumen obstruction. The second, or more distal, injection creates a mound, altering the anatomy and configuration of the ureteral orifice.

retrieved_docs.title: Vesicoureteral Reflux -- Technique or Treatment -- Techniques of Endoscopic Vesicorureteral Surgery
retrieved_docs.content: Three different basic techniques can be used in the endoscopic application of bulking agents—the suburethral transurethral injection (STING) technique, the hydrodistension implantation (HIT) method, and the combined proximal/distal intraluminal injections (double-HIT) procedure.

retrieved_docs.title: Vesicoureteral Reflux -- Technique or Treatment -- Techniques of Endoscopic Vesicorureteral Surgery
retrieved_docs.content: More material is required for the injection, but this also increases the reported success rate to an average of 93%. [163] However, additional punctures through the intramural ureteral mucosa may result in leakage of the bulking agent and eventually require an additional endoscopic periureteral bulking procedure. Among the various endoscopic procedures described, the double-HIT is the most commonly utilized method for endoscopic VUR surgery in the United States, possibly being the most effective technique, with an overall resolution of VUR of 82.5% compared to 71.4% with STING. [164] [165] [166]

retrieved_docs.title: Vesicoureteral Reflux -- Technique or Treatment -- Endoscopic Treatment of Vesicoureteral Reflux
retrieved_docs.content: Polyacrylate polyalcohol copolymer: Introduced in 2010, polyacrylate polyalcohol copolymer is a synthetic, non-biodegradable periureteral bulking agent used for endoscopic periureteral injections to treat VUR. The copolymer features a relatively large microsphere size of 320 μm, minimizing particle migration. The material's non-biodegradability ensures greater stability and long-term efficacy. No apparent malignancy-promoting effect is observed. The material tends to form a fibrous capsule with significant scarring. Severe inflammation, scarring, and fibrosis typically develop at the injection site, potentially leading to ureteral obstruction long after administration. This outcome is attributed to a persistent foreign body reaction, the use of alcohol polymers, and the relatively large microsphere size. The treatment may result in ureteral obstruction over time, necessitating long-term follow-up. No evidence suggests a malignancy-promoting effect.

retrieved_docs.title: Vesicoureteral Reflux -- Technique or Treatment -- Standard Non-Endoscopic Ureteral Reimplantation Antireflux Surgeries
retrieved_docs.content: The location of the detrusor muscle tunnel should be outlined with superficial electrocautery markings while the bladder is fully distended, starting from the UVJ and extending along the planned course of the ureter. The ureteral diameter to the tunnel length ratio of 5 or more to 1 should be followed. Clinicians should be aware that a tunnel length of 3 cm is generally sufficient and will probably work adequately in most patients. A 4-0 Prolene suture on a straight cutting needle is placed intraperitoneally as an anchoring suture, with the external end positioned laterally to the midline port to provide appropriate tension for working on the detrusor tunnel. [209]

retrieved_docs.content: catheterization is the parent concept for a range of ureter‑related endoscopic procedures, including cystoscopic catheterization of the ureter, transurethral cystoscopy, ureteral injections and implantations, ureteroscopic dilatation, and bilateral or unilateral endoscopic ureteric catheterization with fluoroscopic imaging of the upper urinary tract. “Procedures on ureter” are defined as the inverse of transurethral excision, drainage, or removal of urinary obstruction, and the ureter itself is an indirect procedural site within this hierarchy. Ureteroplication directly targets the ureter, while “URETER: INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS” is classified under “عملية في الحالب” (procedure in the ureter). The group “إجراءات تصوير المسالك البولية” (urinary tract imaging procedures) encompasses urethroscopy (تنظير الإحليل) and endoscopic urinary‑tract procedures such as nephroscopy, electrohydraulic lithotripsy of renal calculi, and renal endoscopy performed through an established nephrostomy

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 143
retrieved_docs.content: Ureteral catheterization is a central category for multiple endoscopic and trans‑urethral interventions on the ureter, including cystoscopic catheterization of the ureter, ureteroscopic dilatation, and bilateral or unilateral endoscopic ureteric catheterization performed with fluoroscopic imaging of the upper urinary tract. These procedures target the ureter as an indirect procedural site, while ureteroplication is a direct ureteral operation. Cystoscopy and other trans‑urethral procedures serve as the parent category for ureteral catheterization and give rise to the child procedure “transurethral excision, drainage, or removal of urinary obstruction,” which is also classified under “procedure in the ureter” (عملية في الحالب) and linked to URETER: INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS. The broader group “Procedures on Ureter” encompasses both ureteral catheterization and transurethral excision, drainage, or removal of urinary obstruction, reflecting their overlapping therapeutic roles. These endoscopic catheterizations are also mapped to Other x‑ray of the urinary system because they rely on fluoroscopic guidance. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 203
retrieved_docs.content: Ureteral catheterization is a central procedural category that encompasses a range of endoscopic and imaging‑guided interventions on the ureter and adjacent urinary structures. It includes cystoscopic catheterization of the ureter, which is a specific type within the broader parent group “Cystoscopy and other transurethral procedures,” and is related to the child procedure “Transurethral excision; drainage; or removal of urinary obstruction.” The higher‑level set “Procedures on Ureter” contains ureteral catheterization and the same transurethral excision procedure, indicating their close connection. Derived interventions from ureteral catheterization comprise ureteroscopic dilatation of the ureter and bilateral or unilateral endoscopic ureteric catheterization performed under fluoroscopic guidance; the latter are also mapped from “Other x‑ray of the urinary system.” Anatomically, the ureter serves as an indirect procedure site for ureteral catheterization, while the renal pelvis

Interventional radiology procedures that combine contrast agents with fibrinolytic drugs are employed for endovascular thrombolysis, fluoroscopic‑guided thrombolysis of intracranial vessels, and percutaneous thrombolysis of central venous catheters, providing vascular visualization and targeted drug delivery. Urological interventions are organized hierarchically: ureteral catheterization encompasses cystoscopic ureteral catheterization, ureteroscopic dilatation, ureter injections and implantations, and endoscopic ureteric catheterization with fluoroscopic imaging of the upper urinary tract (unilateral or bilateral). These procedures are grouped with other X‑ray examinations of the urinary system and are performed in the kidney‑pelvis region, which also serves as the indirect target for percutaneous nephrostomy tube insertion and for transurethral excision, drainage, or removal of urinary obstruction. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 199
retrieved_docs.content: INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS” and transurethral excision, drainage, or removal of urinary obstruction are categorized as “procedure in the ureter,” targeting the ureteral lumen while potentially affecting the renal pelvis indirectly. Imaging of the upper urinary tract is provided by intravenous urography (Urography/pyelography, with or without KUB or tomography), known clinically as the intravenous pyelogram, which is classified as a “urinary tract image.” The intravenous pyelogram visualizes the renal pelvis and the collecting system, supporting diagnosis, procedural planning, and follow‑up. Together, these hierarchical classifications, anatomical relationships, and imaging modalities delineate how ureteral and renal pelvis procedures are organized, how they interrelate, and how clinicians can select appropriate interventions and imaging strategies for the management of obstructive urologic conditions and other urinary tract disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 130
retrieved_docs.content: Ureteral catheterization is the central procedural concept linking a spectrum of endoscopic and imaging‑guided interventions within the urinary tract. It includes cystoscopic catheterization of the ureter, ureteroscopic dilatation, and bilateral or unilateral endoscopic ureteric catheterization performed under fluoroscopic guidance, all of which are mapped to the radiographic category “Other x‑ray of the urinary system.” These catheter‑based techniques are grouped with “Cystoscopy and other transurethral procedures,” under which transurethral excision, drainage, or removal of urinary obstruction is classified. Both the ureteral catheterization and the transurethral obstruction‑removal procedures are direct interventions on the ureter, and they share the child procedure “URETER: INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS” (Arabic “عملية في الحالب”). Ureteroplication is another direct ureteral operation, reinforcing the ureter as a primary procedural site. The renal pelvis (Czech “ledvinná pánvička”)

Conversely, “Procedures on Ureter” is also the parent of the transurethral excision, drainage, or removal of urinary obstruction, linking these obstruction‑relieving interventions to the same overall procedural family. “Cystoscopy and other transurethral procedures” is another parent node for ureteral catheterization and further contains the transurethral excision, drainage, or removal of urinary obstruction as a child activity, emphasizing the close relationship between cystoscopic approaches and obstruction management. Additional ureter‑focused interventions include ureteroplication, which directly targets the ureter, and renal endoscopy performed through an established nephrostomy or pyelostomy that incorporates ureteral catheterization and ureteral dilation as part of the technique. In Arabic, “عملية في الحالب” (procedure in the ureter) is a parent of “URETER: INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS,” which in turn parents ureteral catheterization, integrating injection and implantation techniques into the overall framework. Collectively, these hierarchical classifications, procedural mappings, and anatomical relationships form a comprehensive network of ureter‑focused catheterization, dilation, imaging, and excision procedures essential for accurate coding, clinical decision‑making, and coordinated patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 707
retrieved_docs.content: Ureteroplication is a surgical procedure performed directly on the ureter, targeting this tubular structure to correct or modify its function. Similarly, renal endoscopy conducted through an established nephrostomy or pyelostomy—accompanied by ureteral catheterization and dilation of the ureter—also involves direct manipulation of the ureter. Both interventions are urologic techniques that focus on the ureter, highlighting its central role in procedures aimed at diagnosing, treating, or managing ureteral abnormalities and related renal conditions.

Overall, these interrelated concepts illustrate a comprehensive framework in which various endoscopic, fluoroscopic, and transurethral methods are organized under the umbrella of ureteral catheterization and broader ureteral procedures, facilitating consistent documentation, coding, and clinical decision‑making for patient care involving the upper urinary tract.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 451
retrieved_docs.content: Ureteral catheterization is a core category of procedures performed on the ureter, encompassing a range of endoscopic and imaging‑guided techniques. Specific interventions that are classified as ureteral catheterization include cystoscopic catheterization of ureter, ureteroscopic dilatation of ureter, and endoscopic ureteric catheterization with fluoroscopic imaging of the upper urinary tract (both bilateral and unilateral). These procedures are also linked to “URETER: INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS,” which is recognized as a sub‑type of ureteral catheterization and is described in Arabic as عملية في الحالب. Ureteral catheterization itself is a subtype of the broader “Procedures on Ureter” hierarchy, and it is further situated within the larger domain of transurethral excision, drainage, or removal of urinary obstruction.
